summary: Richard S. Sutton argues that Large Language Models lack true intelligence because they do not possess substantive goals regarding the external world. He distinguishes between next-token prediction—which doesn't influence the environment—and true goal-oriented behavior, while also contrasting purely computational tasks like math with the necessity of learning empirical consequences in the physical world.

principles.statement: Intelligence is fundamentally defined by the ability to achieve goals.
principles.rationale: A system without goals is merely a 'behaving system.' True intelligence requires the computational capacity to enact change and achieve specific outcomes.
principles.source_quote: For me, having a goal is the essence of intelligence, right? Something is intelligent if it can achieve goals.

principles.statement: Next-token prediction is not a substantive goal.
principles.rationale: Predicting tokens does not influence the external world; it only determines what the model should say next, rather than predicting how the world will respond to its actions.
principles.source_quote: You can't look at a system and say, "Oh, it uh has a goal if it's just sitting there predicting and being happy with itself that it's predicting accurately."

principles.statement: Modeling the physical world requires learning empirical consequences, which is fundamentally different from solving math problems.
principles.rationale: Math is purely computational and akin to standard planning, whereas navigating the physical world requires an agent to actively learn the consequences of its actions through experience.
principles.source_quote: The empirical world has to be learned. You have to learn the consequences. Um whereas the uh the math is is more just computational. It's more like standard planning.

mental_models.name: Empirical vs. Computational Domains
mental_models.description: A distinction between environments where rules and consequences must be learned through interaction (the physical/empirical world) and environments where rules are predefined and success relies on search and planning (mathematics).
mental_models.example: Explaining why LLMs can achieve gold at the Math Olympiad (computational planning) but still lack the ability to model the physical world (empirical learning).

mental_models.name: Intelligence as Goal Achievement
mental_models.description: Viewing intelligence not merely as complex behavior or pattern matching, but specifically as the computational part of the ability to achieve goals in an environment (borrowing from John McCarthy's definition).

signature_quotes.text: For me, having a goal is the essence of intelligence, right? Something is intelligent if it can achieve goals.
signature_quotes.context: Defining what separates true intelligence from mere behaving systems.

signature_quotes.text: Next token is what they should say, what the action should be. It's not what the world will give them in response to what they do.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why next-token prediction is not a true prediction of the world.

signature_quotes.text: You can't look at a system and say, "Oh, it uh has a goal if it's just sitting there predicting and being happy with itself that it's predicting accurately."
signature_quotes.context: Arguing that LLMs lack substantive goals about the external world.

signature_quotes.text: The empirical world has to be learned. You have to learn the consequences. Um whereas the uh the math is is more just computational.
signature_quotes.context: Contrasting the success of LLMs in math with their inability to model the physical world.

anti_patterns.description: Equating next-token prediction with having a substantive goal or making true predictions about the world.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Next-token prediction only dictates the model's immediate output (what it should say), not what the external world will return in response to an action. It lacks a feedback loop of influencing and being surprised by the environment.
